name: PyO3 + maturin
summary: Cross-language packaging stack: PyO3 (David Hewitt et al., since 2017) is the canonical Rust crate for writing Python extension modules via #[pyclass]/#[pymethods]/#[pyfunction] macros; maturin (Konstantin Stadler, since 2018) is the build tool that packages PyO3-based crates into Python wheels following PEP 517/518. Together they shifted Python's extension language from C/C++ to Rust within five years.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: **PyO3** (David Hewitt et al.; ecosystem maintained collaboratively, since 2017) is the canonical Rust crate for writing Python extension modules; **maturin** (Konstantin Stadler, since 2018) is the build tool that packages PyO3-based crates into Python wheels (`.whl`) following PEP 517/518. Together they make Rust the primary language for new performance-critical Python extensions, displacing the historical C/C++ + setuptools stack.

sections.title: PyO3 macro surface and ABI3
sections.content:
**PyO3 macro surface** — `#[pyclass]` annotates a Rust struct as a Python class; `#[pymethods]` annotates an `impl` block as a Python method table; `#[pyfunction]` exposes a Rust function to Python. The macros emit FFI glue against Python's C API at compile time.

**ABI3 mode** (`abi3-py38` feature) targets the stable Python ABI so a single wheel works for Python 3.8+ across CPython versions. Without ABI3, a wheel is bound to a specific CPython minor version (`cp310` only works on Python 3.10); with ABI3, one `.whl` covers Python 3.8 through 3.12+ on the same platform.

sections.title: maturin build modes and wheel distribution
sections.content:
**maturin build modes** — `develop` for in-place editable installs during development; `build` for wheel production; `publish` for uploading to PyPI; `sdist` for source-distribution archives. Cross-compilation to Linux/macOS/Windows via `maturin build --target` and `--release` flags.

**PyPI distribution** — wheels built by maturin are statically-compiled native extensions; users `pip install` them and the binary runs without a Rust toolchain on the consumer's machine. The wheel naming (`mypackage-0.1.0-cp310-cp310-linux_x86_64.whl`) encodes the Python version + ABI + platform so pip selects the right wheel automatically.

sections.title: Ecosystem reach
sections.content:
The ecosystem reach is significant. Major Python packages with PyO3-based Rust extensions include:

- **Polars** — DataFrame library, faster than pandas for large datasets.
- **Pydantic v2** — validation core rewritten from Cython to Rust.
- **cryptography** — the canonical Python cryptography library, mostly Rust as of 2022.
- **tokenizers** — Hugging Face tokenisation library.
- **ruff** — Python linter rewriting flake8+isort+pyupgrade in Rust.
- **uv** — Astral's pip+venv replacement.
- **orjson** — fast JSON serialisation.

PyPI ships hundreds of millions of PyO3-based wheel downloads per month.

sections.title: Distinct from Cython, cffi, pybind11
sections.content:
Distinct from older C-extension toolchains (`Cython`, `cffi`, `pybind11` for C++): PyO3 + maturin make the *cross-language packaging* concern transparent. A Python user `pip install`s a package without knowing Rust is involved, and wheel selection automatically picks the native binary for the user's platform.

The cost is that source distributions require a Rust toolchain to build, which can complicate hermetic-build pipelines (`data/nix-flakes`, `data/guix`); maturin's wheel-only distribution mitigates this for most consumers.

sections.title: Design lesson — cross-language extension packaging
sections.content:
The lesson generalises: **a high-quality cross-language packaging toolchain can shift an ecosystem's "extension language" choice within a few years**. Python's extension language was C/C++ for 30 years; PyO3 + maturin shifted it to Rust within five.

The success conditions are:

1. The systems language has a stable C ABI to target.
2. The build tool fits the host language's existing packaging conventions (PEP 517/518 in this case).
3. The new extensions ship binary distributions so end users don't need the new toolchain.

For language designers building a host language with C-ABI-compatible FFI, this is the cleanest production template for cross-language extension packaging.
